best_for: Distributed systems, public-facing IDs you don't want to reveal sequence/volume of, or multi-tenant apps merging data from multiple sources
avoid_when: High-volume tables where the extra 12 bytes vs a bigint and worse index locality genuinely matter at scale
tradeoffs: Non-guessable and generatable client-side before insert (useful for optimistic UI), but larger and less index-friendly than sequential integers

best_for: Internal tables where insertion order and compact indexes matter more than ID unguessability
avoid_when: IDs are ever exposed publicly and you don't want competitors/users inferring row counts or growth rate
tradeoffs: Smallest, fastest-indexing PK type, but sequential and guessable — never use as the only access-control mechanism for a resource

option: UUID PK with a Separate Public Slug/Short-ID
best_for: Public-facing resources needing both safe internal joins (UUID) and a short, shareable identifier (slug or nanoid) in URLs
avoid_when: Internal-only tables with no public URL surface — the extra column is unnecessary
tradeoffs: Gets both worlds — DB-internal UUID stability and a clean public identifier — at the cost of maintaining two identifier columns

best_for: Data with compliance/audit requirements, or where 'undo delete' is a real product feature (recoverable trash)
avoid_when: Table grows large and most queries would otherwise need to constantly filter deleted_at IS NULL — index that column if so
tradeoffs: Recoverable and audit-friendly, but every query must remember to filter it out, and unique constraints need care (a 'deleted' row shouldn't block a new row with the same unique value)

best_for: Data with no compliance requirement to retain, or genuinely transient data (sessions, temp uploads)
avoid_when: Any regulatory requirement to retain records, or user-facing 'restore' expectations
tradeoffs: Simpler queries (no filtering needed) and smaller tables over time, but truly unrecoverable — pair with backups as the only recovery path

best_for: Every table, without exception — this is close to a universal default, not a situational choice
avoid_when: A genuinely immutable, append-only log table where 'updated_at' would never be non-null and is just noise — created_at alone is enough there
tradeoffs: Enables debugging, auditing, and simple 'recently changed' queries for free; set updated_at via a DB trigger, not application code, so it can't be forgotten in one code path

best_for: Product attributes that vary wildly by category (e.g. e-commerce variants), user preferences blobs, or webhook payload archival
avoid_when: Fields that are queried/filtered/joined on frequently and consistently across all rows — those belong as real typed columns, not buried in JSONB
tradeoffs: Schema flexibility without a migration per new field, and Postgres JSONB supports GIN indexing for reasonably fast queries — but loses type safety and makes ad-hoc reporting harder

best_for: Core business entities where fields are consistent, frequently queried/filtered/joined, and benefit from DB-level type constraints
avoid_when: Attributes genuinely vary per row in ways no fixed schema captures well
tradeoffs: Type safety, indexability, and constraint enforcement (NOT NULL, CHECK) at the DB level — the right default until you hit a real case for JSONB

best_for: A small, genuinely fixed set of values that rarely changes (e.g. `order_status`: pending/paid/shipped/cancelled)
avoid_when: Values will be added/removed/reordered by end users or admins at runtime, or the list is large/dynamic — use a lookup table instead
tradeoffs: Type-safe and self-documenting in the schema, but adding a value is a migration and Postgres can't easily remove/reorder enum values once used

best_for: Value sets that admins/users can extend over time (categories, tags, custom statuses per tenant)
avoid_when: A truly fixed, small, code-referenced set — a lookup table adds a join for no benefit there
tradeoffs: Extensible without a schema migration and can carry metadata (display name, color, sort order) per value, at the cost of an extra join

best_for: Any true many-to-many relationship (users↔roles, posts↔tags, products↔categories)
avoid_when: One side is actually 1-to-many — don't add join-table complexity for a relationship that's really just a foreign key
tradeoffs: Standard, well-indexed pattern; add a composite unique constraint on the two foreign keys to prevent duplicate associations

best_for: A comments/attachments/activity-log table that needs to reference many different resource types (post, product, user) generically
avoid_when: The set of referenceable types is small and stable — separate foreign key columns (nullable) or separate join tables per type are more DB-integrity-safe
tradeoffs: Avoids a join table per referenceable type, but the database can't enforce a real foreign-key constraint across a polymorphic reference — integrity is app-enforced only

best_for: Simple, shallow hierarchies (categories, org charts) where you mostly query 'direct children' or 'direct parent'
avoid_when: Deep trees where you frequently need 'all descendants' or 'all ancestors' in one query — that requires recursive CTEs here, which get expensive at depth
tradeoffs: Simplest to implement and update (move a node = update one row), but full-subtree queries need a recursive CTE

best_for: Deep or frequently-queried hierarchies (comment threads, nested categories) where 'all descendants of X' needs to be fast and simple
avoid_when: Simple two-or-three-level hierarchies where adjacency list's simplicity is preferable
tradeoffs: Postgres's native `ltree` type makes ancestor/descendant queries fast with a GiST index, at the cost of a path column that must be kept in sync on moves

best_for: SaaS apps on Supabase/Postgres where tenant isolation should be enforced at the database layer, not just in application code (see BE032)
avoid_when: Tenants need genuinely separate backup/restore schedules, independent scaling, or contractual data-residency guarantees per tenant
tradeoffs: One schema to migrate and maintain; RLS makes 'a query can only ever see its own tenant's rows' a database-enforced guarantee instead of an app-layer convention that can be forgotten in one endpoint

best_for: Mid-size B2B SaaS with a bounded, known tenant count where per-tenant customization or stronger isolation is worth the operational cost
avoid_when: Large numbers of tenants (hundreds+) — migrating N schemas on every deploy becomes the bottleneck
tradeoffs: Stronger isolation and easier per-tenant backup/restore than shared tables, but every migration must run against every tenant schema

best_for: Enterprise contracts requiring full data isolation, independent scaling, or data residency per customer
avoid_when: Most SaaS products, especially early-stage — this is the most operationally expensive isolation model and is rarely justified before real enterprise contracts require it
tradeoffs: Maximum isolation and independent scaling per tenant, at meaningfully higher infrastructure and migration-orchestration cost

option: tsvector Column + GIN Index for Full-Text Search
best_for: In-app search over text content (posts, products, docs) where a dedicated search service (Elasticsearch/Algolia) would be overkill
avoid_when: Search needs typo-tolerance, faceting, or relevance tuning beyond what Postgres full-text search offers — reach for a dedicated search service instead
tradeoffs: Full-text search without new infrastructure, generated/maintained via a trigger or generated column, good enough for most in-app search until you outgrow it

option: B-tree Index on Foreign Keys and Frequent WHERE/JOIN Columns
best_for: The default index type for equality/range lookups — apply to every foreign key column and every column used in WHERE/JOIN/ORDER BY
avoid_when: A column that's rarely queried directly — indexing everything 'just in case' slows down writes for no read benefit
tradeoffs: Postgres does not auto-index foreign key columns — this is one of the most common missed-index causes of slow joins in production

best_for: Queries that always filter on a condition (e.g. `WHERE deleted_at IS NULL`, `WHERE status = 'active'`) — index only the rows that matter
avoid_when: The filtered condition isn't stable/known in advance, or most rows match it anyway (little size benefit)
tradeoffs: Smaller, faster index than indexing the whole column, tailored exactly to your hot-path query's WHERE clause

best_for: Multi-column WHERE/ORDER BY queries — put the most selective / most frequently equality-filtered column first
avoid_when: A composite index where the query pattern doesn't consistently use the leading column — it won't be used efficiently then
tradeoffs: Column order determines which query patterns can use the index (leftmost-prefix rule) — get it wrong and the index silently goes unused

option: GIN Index for JSONB / Array / Full-Text Columns
best_for: Querying inside a JSONB column, array containment checks, or full-text search (tsvector) — B-tree can't index these efficiently
avoid_when: Simple equality/range lookups on scalar columns — B-tree is the right (and default) choice there
tradeoffs: Makes JSONB/array/full-text queries fast, but GIN indexes are larger and slower to update than B-tree — don't add them speculatively

option: Foreign Key ON DELETE Behavior (CASCADE vs RESTRICT vs SET NULL)
best_for: Every foreign key — decide explicitly per relationship: CASCADE for true ownership (delete a post's comments with it), RESTRICT to prevent accidental orphaning of important records, SET NULL for optional references
avoid_when: Never leave this as an unconsidered default — the default in most ORMs/migration tools is RESTRICT/NO ACTION, which silently blocks deletes if not chosen deliberately
tradeoffs: Getting this wrong either orphans data silently (no cascade when you needed one) or blocks a delete operation mysteriously (restrict when you needed cascade)

best_for: Invariants that must always hold regardless of which application code path writes the row (e.g. `price >= 0`, `end_date > start_date`)
avoid_when: Validation that's genuinely business-logic-dependent and changes per context — that belongs in application code, not a rigid DB constraint
tradeoffs: Database-enforced invariants can't be bypassed by a bug in one code path or a direct SQL fix that skips application validation

option: Unique Constraint at the Database Level (not just app-checked)
best_for: Any uniqueness requirement (email, slug, SKU) — enforce it as a DB unique constraint/index, never rely solely on an app-level 'check then insert'
avoid_when: Never skip the DB-level constraint even if app-level validation also exists — a race condition between two concurrent requests can defeat app-only checks
tradeoffs: App-level 'check then insert' has a race condition window; a DB unique constraint is the only way to guarantee it under concurrent writes

best_for: Audit trails, activity feeds, or event-sourcing-style systems where you need an immutable history of what happened, not just current state
avoid_when: The product only ever needs current state, with no audit/history requirement
tradeoffs: Rows are never updated or deleted, only inserted — trivial to reason about and replay, but current-state queries require either a separate materialized view or reducing the log on read

best_for: Any resource with a public detail page where a human-readable URL (`/posts/how-to-ship-fast`) is preferable to a raw ID
avoid_when: Purely internal/API-only resources with no public URL surface
tradeoffs: Better UX and SEO than a raw UUID/ID in the URL, at the cost of needing a uniqueness strategy (append a suffix on collision) and handling slug changes without breaking old links

best_for: Any column storing a monetary amount — store cents (or the smallest unit of the currency), never a float
avoid_when: Never store money as a floating-point type — floating-point rounding errors compound in financial calculations
tradeoffs: Integer cents avoids floating-point rounding errors entirely; format to a display currency string only at the presentation layer, never store the formatted string

option: Status/State Column Backed by an Explicit State Machine
best_for: Any resource with a lifecycle (order: pending→paid→shipped→delivered) — define the allowed transitions explicitly in application code, backed by an enum/lookup column for the current state
avoid_when: A field that's just a flag with no lifecycle/transition rules — that's a plain boolean or enum, not a state machine
tradeoffs: Prevents invalid transitions (shipped→pending) from ever being written, and makes 'what statuses exist and how do they flow' reviewable in one place instead of scattered across the codebase

option: Row-Level Security Policies Colocated with Table Migrations
best_for: Supabase/Postgres projects using RLS — write and version the RLS policy in the same migration file that creates or alters the table it protects
avoid_when: A table that intentionally has no RLS because it's accessed only via a trusted server-side service role — document that choice explicitly rather than leaving it ambiguous
tradeoffs: Keeps the access-control rule next to the schema it governs instead of drifting apart in a separate, easy-to-forget migration
